19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Phenomenology & the Brentano School

Wolfgang Köhler

1887 – 1967

Gestalt form, insight, and a non-reductive philosophy of perception.

The central conclusion is that Wolfgang Köhler gives the materials for a unified, non-Cartesian philosophy of mind: consciousness is a structured phenomenal field, not a private heap of sensations; mind is not an immaterial substance outside nature; and yet the mind cannot be captured by a reductive vocabulary that ignores organization, expression, insight, value, and normativity. His mature view is best described as phenomenological Gestalt naturalism with axiological realism: a naturalistic monism that begins from direct experience, treats mental life as dynamically organized, and insists that “rightness,” “wrongness,” “demand,” and “requiredness” are not dispensable subjective ornaments but belong to the structure of the world as experienced and, ultimately, to nature itself. Köhler’s theological relevance lies precisely here: he does not revive the soul as an immaterial theological substance, but he tries to preserve—inside a scientific worldview—the very phenomena that soul-language and religious moral language historically protected: selfhood, inwardness, purposive order, moral demand, and the difference between a world of brute facts and a world in which some things genuinely ought or ought not to be.

I will treat Köhler’s own works as primary: The Mentality of Apes, Gestalt Psychology, and especially The Place of Value in a World of Facts. I also draw on English-language scholarship and on Spanish, French, and German reception, since the non-Anglophone literature often sees more clearly that Köhler is not merely a perception theorist but a philosopher of nature, value, and mind. The Spanish article by José María Gondra is especially useful for Köhler’s value theory; Matthias Heuser’s French study is useful for the Wittgenstein/Köhler and body-expression context; and German-language discussion such as Norbert Bischof’s work keeps alive the problem of psychophysical isomorphism, freedom, and the explanation gap.

1. Köhler’s project: not psychology alone, but a philosophy of mind and nature

Köhler is often remembered for chimpanzee insight, Gestalt perception, and the anti-associationist slogan that wholes are not mere sums of parts. That summary is not wrong, but it is too small. Ulric Neisser’s biographical memoir emphasizes that Köhler’s deepest commitments were theoretical and philosophical: psychophysical isomorphism, the relation between brain and mind, opposition to positivism and mechanistic psychology, and a broader attempt to show that mind belongs to nature without being flattened into atomistic mechanism. Neisser also notes that Köhler’s work had roots in Husserlian phenomenology and Kantian concerns, while resisting vitalism, positivism, and crude mechanism.

Köhler himself presents The Place of Value in a World of Facts as a philosophical work, not as a narrow psychological treatise. He explicitly rejects rigid boundaries between science and philosophy and says the procedure is not positivistic; it must return to the sources of concepts and carry out a qualitative analysis of experience. This is crucial: Köhler is not trying to add “values” to science from outside, nor to retreat from science into theology. He is trying to show that the scientific image has been artificially narrowed by a bad conception of fact.

The problem, for Köhler, is that modern scientific culture often treats the world as a collection of neutral facts and then cannot explain why human beings experience anything as meaningful, rationally demanded, morally required, beautiful, ugly, right, or wrong. In his Harvard lectures he frames this as a cultural crisis: people ask questions of meaning and value, while many professors fence off questions of value, moral judgment, and metaphysical interpretation as if they were not intellectually respectable. His worry is not anti-scientific; it is that a falsely restricted science abandons the deepest human questions to irrational “other teachers.”

This gives the theological dimension its proper place. Köhler is not doing theology in the doctrinal sense. He does not infer God, defend immortality, or posit a spiritual substance. But he takes seriously the kind of existential and moral problem to which theology responds: whether the world contains more than indifferent happenings, whether reason and value have standing, and whether the human person can be understood without being mutilated by mechanistic categories. His answer is a secular, naturalistic, Gestalt answer, but it does not dismiss the theological problem as a pseudo-problem.

2. Method: direct experience is not an embarrassment but the ground of science

Köhler’s philosophy of mind begins with a methodological reversal. Behaviorism and positivism wanted psychology to avoid “consciousness” and rely on publicly observable behavior. Köhler argues that this is confused, because all physical observation and measurement ultimately depend on direct experience. A physicist reading an instrument, seeing a pointer, hearing a sound, inspecting a line, or recording a number is still relying on experience. Scientific exactness is not achieved by escaping experience; it is achieved by disciplining and organizing it.

This is not “introspectionism” in the old technical sense. Köhler rejects the idea that psychology must begin with artificial introspective training. He asks instead for simple, careful, phenomenological description: what is actually given in ordinary experience before it is distorted by theory. He argues that if direct experience can guide the construction of the external physical world, then it can also provide hints about the physiological processes underlying experience.

This point is decisive for reconstructing his philosophy of mind. Köhler does not start from the third-person body and then ask how consciousness could be inserted into it. Nor does he start from an isolated Cartesian ego and then ask how an external world could be proven. He starts from the structured field of immediate experience: objects, relations, tensions, movements, bodies, other persons, tasks, obstacles, correctness, error, effort, and demand. Philosophy of mind must begin there because that is where mind is actually encountered.

Yet Köhler’s phenomenological beginning is not idealism. He distinguishes the phenomenal world from the transphenomenal physical world, but he does not deny the latter. Martinelli’s interpretation is helpful here: Köhler develops what can be called an “epistemological dualism,” because phenomenal objects are not simply identical with physical objects; but this is not an ontological dualism of mind-substance and matter-substance. On the contrary, the distinction is meant to support a monistic naturalism in which consciousness depends on, and is structurally correlated with, physical brain processes.

3. Against Cartesianism: mind is not a separate force acting on the body

Köhler’s anti-dualism is explicit. In Gestalt Psychology, he says that direct experience itself is not a force that influences physiological events. The physiological assumptions must explain overt behavior; otherwise one falls into dualism, which he judges unproductive. The important subtlety is that Köhler is not denying the reality of experience. He is denying that experience, considered as a separate nonphysical entity, pushes the body around from outside the causal order.

This yields a distinctive view of mental causation. Köhler does not say: first there are physical brain events, then an inert consciousness appears as epiphenomenal shadow. Nor does he say: first there is a soul, then it intervenes in matter. Rather, mental life and brain process are two epistemically different approaches to one organized natural reality. The causal efficacy belongs to the real psychophysical process; the phenomenal side is not an additional ghostly force, but neither is it unreal.

Neisser summarizes Köhler’s fundamental commitment by saying that brain and mind are identical and that the structure of conscious experience mirrors the structure of brain activity. Köhler’s own formulations are often more cautious: he speaks of psychophysical isomorphism as a working hypothesis and as a necessary condition for giving mental life a dynamic physical interpretation. But the direction is clear: he wants a monistic account in which mental organization and physical organization belong together.

This separates him from three rival positions. He opposes Cartesian dualism because it separates mind from nature. He opposes reductive atomism because it destroys the structures actually present in consciousness. And he opposes spiritualistic or vitalistic alternatives because they preserve mind only by making it scientifically mysterious. Martinelli stresses that Köhler’s position is not a plea for spiritualistic, vitalistic, or religious stances, but an attempt to revise the interpretation of physical facts and laws so that requiredness and value can be understood within nature.

4. The body and the “soul”: Köhler’s demystification of body-soul dualism

Köhler’s most direct comments on “soul” are not a doctrine of the soul but a diagnosis of why body-soul dualism became tempting. He notes that for thousands of years the phenomenal body—the body as it appears in experience—was conflated with the organism as a physical, transphenomenal entity. Plato and later religious movements could then picture the body as a prison of the soul, partly because the body as perceived was treated as if it were the physical organism itself.

Köhler’s critique is subtle and worth taking seriously. He does not simply sneer at religious or Platonic language. He says the problem is a confusion between two different tasks. One task concerns the phenomenology of the self and the experienced body: how my body appears, how it is felt, how it belongs to the phenomenal field. Another task concerns the relation between all phenomena and the organism as a transphenomenal physical system. Mind-body philosophy has often gone wrong because it treats the “body” of body-soul talk as if it were already the biological organism described by science.

This is one of Köhler’s most fruitful contributions to philosophy of mind. The “body” that seems alien to spirit is not the same as the living organism studied by physiology. The experienced body may appear heavy, opaque, resistant, vulnerable, or imprisoning; the organism, by contrast, is the dynamic physical system that supports and correlates with the entire phenomenal field. Theologically, this means that the old body/soul opposition is not simply false because “matter wins”; rather, it is based on a phenomenological ambiguity. The soul-language is responding to real experiences of selfhood, interiority, agency, and bodily limitation, but it misidentifies the metaphysical structure of the problem.

A Köhlerian philosophy of mind therefore translates much of traditional “soul” discourse into a theory of phenomenal selfhood, bodily appearance, organismic dynamics, value, and psychophysical structure. It does not say that the soul is a separate substance. It says that what made soul-talk compelling—subjectivity, unity, agency, normativity, and the seeming distance between inner life and the body—must be explained by a more adequate theory of experience and nature.

5. Consciousness as a Gestalt field, not a mosaic of sensations

The first positive thesis of Köhler’s philosophy of consciousness is anti-atomism. Consciousness is not built from independent sensations plus external associations. It is an organized field of parts and wholes, in which the character of a part depends on its place in the whole. Köhler’s standard examples include melodies, visual forms, apparent motion, and problem situations. A melody is not a sum of isolated tones; the tones function as parts of a structured whole.

In The Place of Value, Köhler gives three major Gestalt traits: the world is not a mosaic or mere continuum; there are units with context-dependent properties; and the properties of parts are determined by their context. He uses the melody example to show that a vector or requiredness cannot exist in isolation, any more than a fish can exist out of water. This is not merely an aesthetic point. It is a general ontology of mental phenomena: mental contents are field-terms, not atoms.

The French reception brings this out well. Heuser describes Gestalt theory as extending beyond psychology into physics, biology, anthropology, aesthetics, ethics, and cognitive science, with the primacy of organization as its guiding idea. He emphasizes that perception, action, and expression form an integrated field, and that value and Prägnanz are not subjective projections added to neutral objects but integral to contextual objects.

This means Köhler’s consciousness is neither a Cartesian theater nor a Humean bundle. It is a dynamic organization in which figure and ground, tension and resolution, part and whole, self and environment, task and obstacle, correctness and error appear together. Consciousness is already worldly and relational before reflection begins.

6. Psychophysical isomorphism: the bridge between phenomenal structure and brain structure

Köhler’s central mind-body hypothesis is psychophysical isomorphism. The claim is not that a conscious triangle is literally a tiny triangle in the brain, nor that mental space maps geometrically onto cortical space. Köhler explicitly says that isomorphism is topological and functional, not pictorial or metrically geometrical. Relations such as inside, outside, between, contact, separation, and dynamic belonging are the relevant structural relations. Psychophysical distances are functional distances, not simple physical distances in anatomical space.

He defends isomorphism against the criticism that it is a mere verbal trick. For Köhler, it is a substantive hypothesis: certain physical processes, especially in the brain, must have structural and dynamic characteristics corresponding to the structures found in experience. Earlier monisms were too vague because they asserted unity without identifying the kind of physical function that could carry genuine structure.

Martinelli’s reconstruction is especially clear: Köhler’s Physical Gestalten tried to show that Gestalt-like structures are not confined to psychology but exist in the physical universe, especially in field-theoretical and electrochemical systems. The central nervous system could then be understood as capable of Gestalt-like states. Psychological facts and brain events would be similar not in sensory quality but in structure.

This is where Köhler’s physics matters. He was not merely borrowing a metaphor from physics; he wanted to revise psychology by appealing to dynamic fields, equilibrium, forces, and self-organizing systems. Neisser notes that Köhler’s physical field ideas anticipated later interest in self-organizing systems, neural networks, and attractor states, even if the specific cortical current theory did not become accepted neuroscience.

A rigorous reconstruction must therefore separate the durable philosophical thesis from the dated physiological model. The durable thesis is that mental life is organized dynamically and that brain theory must explain organization at the right structural level. The dated part is the particular hypothesis about trans-neuronal electrical currents. Köhler’s philosophical demand remains live even if his specific neurophysiology is superseded: the brain must be understood as a structured, field-like, dynamically organized system if it is to explain structured consciousness.

7. Qualia and the limits of reduction

Köhler is not a crude reductionist. In The Place of Value, he admits that the phenomenal qualities of red, green, and other sensory qualities are not yet physically explained. He also says that mental structures and functions are phenomenologically observable and will not simply be reduced away. What he expects from a dynamic physical theory is not the elimination of experience, but the discovery of physical processes whose structural characteristics correspond to mental operations.

This is important for contemporary philosophy of mind. Köhler’s position is neither eliminativism nor standard property dualism. He accepts an explanatory gap concerning qualia, but he does not infer a metaphysical gap between soul and body. He treats the gap as a scientific and conceptual problem within a monistic framework. The existence of unexplained phenomenal quality does not license Cartesian substance dualism; it shows that physics and neurophysiology must become adequate to the structures they are supposed to explain.

The German reception remains sensitive to this difficulty. Bischof discusses the Leib-Seele problem in terms of psychophysical isomorphism and parallelity, noting the pressure points around freedom, closed natural causality, and the question of why physical processes are accompanied by consciousness at all. That is a fair pressure on Köhler: isomorphism is powerful as a structural thesis, but it does not by itself dissolve every hard problem concerning consciousness, time, and freedom.

A charitable but rigorous verdict is this: Köhler gives a strong anti-dualistic framework for relating conscious structure to brain structure, but he does not fully solve the qualitative character problem. His answer is programmatic and methodological: begin with phenomenological structures, reject atomism, seek corresponding dynamic neural structures, and avoid premature metaphysical inflation.

8. Intentionality and “transcendence”: consciousness exceeds what is immediately present

One of Köhler’s most philosophically interesting moves is his treatment of transcendence. In ordinary consciousness, what is given often points beyond itself: a remembered name almost present but not yet retrieved, an unfinished task, a missing part, a wrong chord demanding correction, an object partly hidden, an absent but relevant relation. Consciousness is not sealed inside its immediate sensory contents. It has horizons, references, traces, absences, and demands.

Martinelli emphasizes that Köhler’s “transcendence” is not mystical or religious transcendence. It refers to the way phenomenal contexts depend on and point toward extraphenomenal realities, especially physiological supports such as memory traces. The “tip of the tongue” phenomenon is a good example: the phenomenal field contains a felt direction toward something not yet present, and Köhler interprets this as involving neural traces that participate in the present context.

Köhler himself argues that some contexts extend beyond present phenomena into traces, and that some traces participate in contexts in which things are right or wrong. This is remarkable. It means that memory and normativity are not merely subjective episodes; they can belong to larger psychophysical contexts that include present experience and retained physical traces.

This gives Köhler a naturalized theory of intentionality. Consciousness can be about what is absent, incomplete, wrong, remembered, or demanded because the phenomenal field is not an isolated surface. It is functionally embedded in wider psychophysical organization. A soul-theory might explain this by saying the soul ranges beyond the body. Köhler explains it by saying that phenomenal contexts and neural traces belong to one organized natural process.

9. Insight: intelligence as grasp of structure

Köhler’s famous ape experiments are central to his philosophy of mind because they show that intelligence is not mere association or trial and error. In The Mentality of Apes, he asks whether chimpanzees can behave with intelligence and insight, partly because they are close to human beings in body chemistry and brain structure. The theoretical aim is to understand relatively primitive intelligent performances as a starting point for a theory of intelligence.

Köhler’s descriptions of successful problem-solving are Gestalt descriptions. A genuine achievement differs from accidental success because it appears as a continuous, unified occurrence: the animal suddenly sees the relevant relation and acts in a coherent way. He warns, however, against giving this a supernatural interpretation. Insight is not a miracle; it is the apprehension of the structure of a situation.

He argues against associationist explanations by emphasizing adaptive variations. When a chimpanzee modifies stick use after an obstacle appears, it is implausible to explain every successful variation as a product of accidental histories. Köhler says complete methods of solution often appear suddenly as wholes, and that “good errors” cannot be explained by mere success-selection. Association theory must explain how the animal grasps inner and material relations in the structure of the situation.

This reveals a general theory of thought: thinking is the reorganization or apprehension of a field so that relevant relations become functionally effective. Insight is not an inner picture added to behavior; it is a restructuring of the problem situation as lived and acted in. The “mind” is not a storehouse of associations but a field capable of seeing relations, tensions, detours, obstacles, means, and ends.

Köhler does distinguish humans from chimpanzees, but not by invoking an immaterial rational soul. He emphasizes speech, imagery, and culture. Chimpanzee insight is strongly tied to optical apprehension; human symbolic language and imagery make possible wider temporal organization, tradition, and culture. The difference is profound, but it is not a metaphysical rupture between ensouled humans and mechanical animals.

10. Other minds: expression is directly perceived, not inferred from dead bodies

Köhler also offers an important alternative to the Cartesian problem of other minds. The traditional problem assumes that I see only another body’s movements and must infer an inner mind by analogy with my own case. Köhler rejects the assumptions behind this. He argues that friendliness in a face is not first perceived as a “mere bodily configuration” to which an invisible mental state is then added. The Cartesian split is, in this context, bad philosophy.

For Köhler, the behavior of others can directly present dynamic structures similar to those of inner life. Crescendo, hesitation, tension, anger, friendliness, sadness, effort, and resolution are not hidden behind neutral movements; they are often visible in the expressive form of behavior. The observer’s objective experience can picture essential traits of another’s subjective experience.

This is a major component of Köhler’s philosophy of mind. Mind is not locked inside the skull in the way Cartesianism imagines. It is expressed in bodily dynamics, gestures, posture, voice, face, and action-forms. That does not mean we are infallible about others; expression can mislead. But the basic relation to other minds is not an inference from a soulless body to a hidden soul. It is a perceptual grasp of meaningful embodied organization.

Theologically, this again reframes rather than simply rejects soul-language. If “soul” names the inward life of another person, Köhler’s view says that inwardness is not metaphysically sealed off. The other’s mindedness is not an invisible substance inferred behind matter; it is dynamically expressed in the field of perception and action.

11. Requiredness: the axiological core of Köhler’s philosophy of mind

The deepest and most neglected part of Köhler’s philosophy of mind is his theory of requiredness. In The Place of Value, he argues that human mental life includes intrinsic demands: we experience some conclusions as required, some actions as wrong, some arrangements as fitting or unfitting, some judgments as called for, some corrections as necessary. This is not confined to morality. It appears in logic, aesthetics, practical action, perception, and ethics.

Köhler’s opponent says that science deals only with indifferent facts, while values are subjective preferences, survival devices, or neutral symbols. Köhler replies that this cannot be right, because insight itself involves experienced fitness and lack of fitness. If human mental life were merely a sequence of indifferent facts, then intellectual judgment, moral obligation, aesthetic rightness, and even scientific reasoning would become unintelligible.

This is why he says that at the bottom of human activities there are values and convictions that some things ought to be and others ought not to be. Science itself depends on logical requiredness: evidence requires conclusions, contradictions require rejection, bad arguments demand correction. A science that officially admits only indifferent facts destroys the basis on which its own rational authority depends.

Requiredness is not mere subjective feeling. Köhler says facts themselves issue vectors of acceptance and rejection; subjective requiredness is only a special case. Requiredness has a demanding character, often experienced as “too high,” “too far left,” incomplete, unsuitable, wrong, or needing correction. This makes values field-properties: they arise in structured contexts, not as isolated atomic qualities.

The Spanish reception is especially helpful here. Gondra presents Köhler’s ethical value theory as phenomenological and naturalistic: values are demands required by perceptual contexts and comparable to vectors in physical force fields. Gondra also stresses that Köhler’s moral concerns were not abstract. His Harvard lectures came after his public opposition to Nazism and in the context of a German moral crisis.

Martinelli likewise emphasizes that requiredness shares formal features with physical force: direction, tension, demand, acceptance or rejection, contextual dependence. Köhler’s ambition is to overcome the alleged gap between physical facts and human values, not by reducing value to subjective preference, but by expanding our conception of physical reality and fact.

De Monticelli’s later value-realist reading shows why this remains philosophically important. She treats Köhler’s requiredness as an argument for value realism: values are not merely projected by subjects but can belong to states of affairs as whole-qualities. Wrong, unjust, ugly, incomplete, or disorderly facts can demand correction because the relevant whole has a normative structure.

12. The theological significance of requiredness

Köhler’s requiredness theory is the place where theological concerns deserve the most attention. Religious traditions often insist that the world is not morally indifferent: there is justice and injustice, order and disorder, right and wrong, good and evil. A purely positivistic worldview appears to evacuate that structure and leave value as mere emotion, preference, command, or social convention. Köhler refuses that evacuation.

However, he does not solve the problem by appealing to God, divine command, supernatural law, or an immortal soul. His move is more radical in a philosophical sense: he argues that value and demand are already present in experience and must ultimately be accounted for within nature. Requiredness is not imported from theology; it is phenomenologically discovered. Theology is relevant because it has historically guarded the seriousness of this domain, but Köhler wants a non-theological ontology capable of preserving it.

This means Köhler is not an atheist polemicist in the crude sense, even when he rejects supernatural explanation. His position is better read as a naturalistic transposition of some functions of religious metaphysics. The religious idea that body is not the whole of the person becomes, in Köhler, a distinction between body-percept and organism. The religious idea that the world contains moral demand becomes, in Köhler, the theory of requiredness. The religious idea that spirit is not a mechanical aggregate becomes, in Köhler, Gestalt organization and psychophysical dynamics.

But there is also a limit. Köhler explicitly warns against supernatural interpretation in the case of ape insight, and Martinelli stresses that Köhler’s “transcendence” is not mystical or divine. The theological comments should therefore be taken seriously as part of the problem-field, but Köhler’s own solution is not theological metaphysics. It is a naturalistic, phenomenological, Gestalt reconstruction of the phenomena that theology and soul-language had often claimed as their own.

13. Reconstructing Köhler’s comprehensive philosophy of mind

A comprehensive Köhlerian philosophy of mind can be extracted as the following system.

First, the phenomenological datum thesis: philosophy of mind must begin from direct experience. This is not because experience is private and incorrigible, but because all science, including physics, relies on experienced observations. Direct experience is therefore not a suspicious residue to be eliminated; it is the epistemic starting point.

Second, the field thesis: consciousness is a structured field of Gestalten. Its contents are not independent atoms; they are parts whose character depends on wholes. Perceptual qualities, meanings, tensions, values, and demands arise contextually.

Third, the epistemological dualism thesis: phenomenal objects are not simply identical with physical objects. The experienced body is not the same as the organism described by physiology; experienced space is not the same as physical space; perceived objects are not literally located in the brain. This is an epistemological distinction, not a metaphysical dualism of soul and matter.

Fourth, the ontological monism thesis: despite epistemological dualism, mental life belongs to nature. Köhler’s aim is to correlate phenomenal structures with physical, especially neural, structures. Mind is not a supernatural substance, and direct experience is not an extra causal force acting on the body.

Fifth, the psychophysical isomorphism thesis: the structures of experience and the structures of correlated brain processes correspond topologically and functionally. Isomorphism is not a picture-copy theory; it concerns relations, dynamic organization, and functional structure.

Sixth, the dynamic naturalism thesis: the physical processes relevant to consciousness must be field-like, relational, and self-organizing. Static mechanism and atomistic association cannot explain the organization of perception, thought, and action.

Seventh, the insight thesis: intelligence is the grasp or reorganization of relational structure. Insightful action is not reducible to accumulated associations or random trial-and-error success; it is a field-structured response to the inner relations of a problem situation.

Eighth, the embodied expression thesis: other minds are not primarily inferred from neutral bodily movements. Mental life is often directly perceived in expressive bodily Gestalten. This dissolves, or at least softens, the Cartesian problem of other minds.

Ninth, the trace and transcendence thesis: consciousness is not limited to what is sensorially present. Present experience can point beyond itself through memory traces, absent objects, unfinished structures, and demands for completion. This transcendence is not mystical; it is psychophysical and contextual.

Tenth, the axiological realism thesis: values, demands, and requiredness are not reducible to subjective liking. They are field-properties of structured contexts and are indispensable to logic, ethics, aesthetics, science, and practical life.

Eleventh, the anti-theological-but-theologically-serious soul thesis: Köhler rejects the soul as a separate immaterial substance, but he preserves the phenomena that soul-language tried to name. Selfhood, inwardness, agency, value, and normativity are real; they require better phenomenology and better natural science, not elimination.

Twelfth, the human-continuity thesis: humans are continuous with animals as natural organisms capable of structured experience and intelligent action, but human language, imagery, and culture transform the scale and complexity of mental life.

14. Köhler’s place among major philosophy-of-mind positions

Köhler is not a Cartesian dualist. He rejects the idea that an immaterial mind or soul acts as a separate force on the body. He also rejects the inference-by-analogy model of other minds and the body-as-prison picture when it rests on confusion between experienced body and physical organism.

He is not a behaviorist. He thinks behaviorism’s avoidance of consciousness is methodologically confused because all scientific observation depends on direct experience. He also thinks behaviorism cannot explain insight, expressive perception, or the structured character of mental life.

He is not a reductive materialist in the simple sense. He does not reduce consciousness to atomistic physical events or deny phenomenal structure. He wants a physical theory adequate to the molar, dynamic organization of experience. Epstein and Hatfield’s discussion of Gestalt psychology and philosophy of mind captures this point: Köhler’s psychophysical isomorphism claims that structural properties of the perceptual world resemble properties of correlated brain processes, especially at a molar dynamic level.

He is not an idealist. He distinguishes phenomenal objects from physical objects, but he does not collapse reality into consciousness. The transphenomenal physical world, including the organism and brain, remains indispensable.

He is not a vitalist. He does not posit a special life-force or soul-force to explain organization. Instead, he tries to show that organization is already a legitimate physical and biological category.

The closest contemporary labels would be “non-reductive naturalism,” “structural identity theory,” “phenomenological naturalism,” “dynamic systems philosophy of mind,” and “value-realist Gestalt ontology.” None is exact. Köhler’s distinctive feature is that he unites mind-body theory, perception, intelligence, other minds, and value under one Gestalt principle of structured fields.

15. Strengths of Köhler’s philosophy of mind

Köhler’s first strength is that he avoids the standard false choice between scientific naturalism and respect for lived experience. He does not sacrifice consciousness to behavior, nor nature to soul. His philosophy gives direct experience epistemic priority without making it metaphysically sovereign.

His second strength is his critique of atomism. Many mental phenomena—melody, perception, insight, bodily expression, moral demand—are badly described as sums of independent units. Köhler’s field ontology remains powerful for contemporary work in perception, embodied cognition, ecological psychology, affect theory, and social cognition.

His third strength is his account of insight. By focusing on the grasp of relational structure, Köhler explains why intelligent action can be sudden, coherent, and adaptive without being supernatural. The ape studies remain philosophically important because they undermine both mechanistic associationism and an overly sharp metaphysical divide between humans and animals.

His fourth strength is his treatment of other minds. The idea that expression is directly perceived, not inferred from neutral motion, anticipates later phenomenological and enactive accounts of social cognition. It also offers a strong alternative to skeptical Cartesianism.

His fifth strength is the theory of requiredness. Köhler sees that logic, ethics, aesthetics, and practical life all involve experienced demands. This is a profound challenge to any philosophy that treats normativity as merely subjective. He also sees that science itself cannot justify its norms while officially denying the reality of requiredness.

16. Weaknesses and unresolved problems

The first weakness is physiological specificity. Köhler’s particular cortical field theory did not become the standard neuroscientific account of perception or consciousness. The general demand for dynamic neural organization remains important, but the historical model requires substantial revision. Neisser’s memoir is helpful because it distinguishes Köhler’s enduring commitment to isomorphism and self-organization from the specific scientific fate of his hypotheses.

The second weakness is the qualitative character problem. Köhler acknowledges that phenomenal qualities are not yet physically explained. His structural isomorphism may explain relations, organization, and dynamics better than it explains why red feels like red or pain feels like pain. A Köhlerian can reply that the right physical theory has not yet been achieved, but the gap remains.

The third weakness is the status of requiredness. Köhler argues powerfully that requiredness is experienced as objective and context-bound, but the transition from phenomenological objectivity to full ontological value realism is difficult. Does a wrong chord literally have a value-vector in nature, or is this a structural feature of organism-environment experience? Köhler wants the stronger answer, but the argument requires more development than he gives.

The fourth weakness concerns freedom. If mind and brain are two aspects of one natural process, and if nature is causally closed, how should free action be understood? German discussions of the psychophysical problem show that isomorphic parallelity still faces pressure from questions of determinism, temporal structure, and agency. Köhler gives resources for a compatibilist or field-theoretic account of freedom, but he does not fully elaborate it.

The fifth weakness is theological incompleteness. Köhler can preserve value, inwardness, and selfhood without theological metaphysics, but he does not address the full theological range: grace, sin, divine command, immortality, resurrection, prayer, or the personal God. His relevance to theology is therefore philosophical and anthropological, not doctrinal. He is useful for a theology of mind insofar as he challenges reductive naturalism and body-soul confusion; he is not himself a theologian.

17. The final extracted system: axiological psychophysical Gestalt naturalism

The most accurate name for the philosophy of mind extractable from Köhler is axiological psychophysical Gestalt naturalism.

It is Gestalt because the basic units of mind are structured wholes, not atomic sensations or isolated representations.

It is psychophysical because every phenomenal structure must be correlated with, and ultimately interpreted through, a dynamic physical structure in the organism, especially the brain.

It is naturalist because it rejects supernatural soul-substances, vital forces, and dualistic mental causation.

It is phenomenological because it begins from direct experience and refuses to let scientific abstractions erase the phenomena they are meant to explain.

It is axiological because value and requiredness are not peripheral. They are central to mind. To perceive, think, judge, act, correct, infer, admire, condemn, or understand is to inhabit a field in which some structures fit and others do not, some conclusions are demanded and others rejected, some acts are right and others wrong.

In such a system, the “soul” is not an entity over and above nature. But neither is it dismissed as nonsense. Soul-language becomes an historically important, phenomenologically motivated, but metaphysically misleading way of pointing to the unity, inwardness, value-responsiveness, expressiveness, and agency of the living person. Köhler’s achievement is to show how these phenomena might be taken with full seriousness inside a scientific worldview.

His philosophy of consciousness is therefore not a theory of consciousness alone. It is a theory of the living person in a meaningful world: perceiving structured wholes, acting in problem-fields, encountering others expressively, remembering beyond the present, responding to demands, and belonging to nature without being reduced to a dead aggregate of facts.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
